draft.introduction: The Ahiram sarcophagus inscription from Byblos (c. 1000 BCE) is the oldest known inscription in the Phoenician alphabet, the direct ancestor of Greek, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew scripts. Its brief curse formula protects the royal burial.

translation.text: A coffin which Ithobaal, son of Ahiram, king of Byblos, made for Ahiram his father, as his dwelling for eternity. And if any king or any governor or any commander attacks Byblos and uncovers this coffin, may his scepter of rule be torn away, may the throne of his kingdom be overturned, and may peace flee from Byblos.

translator_notes.note: The Phoenician alphabet, represented here in one of its earliest attestations, is arguably the most consequential invention in human history. By reducing writing to approximately 22 consonantal signs, it democratized literacy beyond the scribal classes who controlled cuneiform and hieroglyphs.
